turns.content: I'm stuck on a production issue where my custom collection type doesn't work with iter.collect() or direct extend. I thought it was just a missing trait, but I can't figure out how to implement the right idiom for this scenario.

It sounds like you are trying to make your own collection compatible with standard library functions. The core issue is likely that you haven't implemented the Extend trait correctly or perhaps confused it with IntoIterator. You should check if your type actually implements Extend and whether the items being collected match the expected element type.

I tried adding impl Extend for my type but iter.collect() still fails, giving me a type mismatch error instead of the compile time help I was hoping for. It feels like I'm missing something fundamental about how this trait interacts with other parts of the standard library.

The problem is often that you are trying to implement Extend without understanding its relationship to IntoIterator and the underlying iterator protocol. You cannot just implement Extend in isolation because functions like iter.collect() rely on a deeper chain of traits including Iterator and specific bounds. You need to ensure your type implements IntoIterator so the standard library can actually iterate over it before collecting.

I see, but I still don't get how Extend fits into this whole picture. If my collection is just a wrapper around Vec, why do I need to implement anything special when I want it to behave like other collections? It seems like too much boilerplate.

You are right that if you are just wrapping Vec without changing behavior, the boilerplate feels unnecessary. However, the real reason you might be in this situation is likely because your wrapper stores data differently or uses a different internal representation than Vec, which breaks the assumption of IntoIterator being trivially derived. You need to implement Extend specifically so that when someone calls extend on an iterator yielding your type, it correctly adds elements without duplicating logic elsewhere.

Okay, I think I understand now, but wait, didn't we say earlier that I just needed IntoIterator? Why is Extend still failing even after adding the trait impl? I'm getting confused about which pieces actually matter here.

You are correct to question this. The confusion arises because many developers mistakenly believe implementing IntoIterator alone satisfies all collection integration needs, but it does not guarantee that your custom type will work seamlessly with extend operations in all contexts. Extend is the specific trait required for the extend pattern so that when you call iter.collect() on an iterator of your type, the standard library can properly add elements to a target container rather than trying to duplicate them.

I think I finally see it now. It wasn't just about making my collection look like Vec; I had to implement Extend so that extend operations work correctly with iter.collect(). This makes sense given how the standard library chains traits together.

Exactly, and you have solved the core problem by understanding the specific role of this idiom in your code. By implementing Extend properly, you ensure that your custom collection behaves consistently with built-in types like Vec or LinkedList when used with iterators and extend operations.
